summary: DHH argues that developers produce better work when making commitments to fellow developers rather than to managers. When accountable only to managers who don't understand technical details, developers cut corners and pad estimates because managers can't tell the difference. Peer accountability naturally enforces honest estimates and higher quality code because other developers can spot shortcuts and appreciate craftsmanship. However, he's skeptical managers will adopt this approach since it requires admitting they lack the technical respect that peers command.
key_points: Technical promises made to peers yield more realistic estimates and higher quality work than promises made to managers
Developers cut corners and pad timelines when accountable to people who can't evaluate the technical details of their work
Peer review creates natural accountability because other developers can spot ugly patches and shortcuts that managers cannot
Adopting peer promises requires managers to admit they don't command the same technical respect as fellow developers, which few will do
money_quotes: Technical promises made to developer peers instead of managers improves the chances that of realistic estimates without excessive padding, motivates people to perform to the best of their abilities, and make them less defensive about their work.
Your peers appreciate how you solve problems and (most) managers do not.
When solving problems for managers - or anyone who doesn't understand or appreciate the details of your work - you're likely to cut corners, let ugly patching slide, and spend time on unrelated work.
If you're smart, your manager won't know the difference anyway.
The managers might now have caught it, but other developers certainly will.
It requires managers to recognize that they don't share the same respect from developers that other developers do. I can't recall any managers willing to do that.
key_insight: Developer accountability works best when directed at peers who can evaluate the craft, not managers who can only evaluate the outcome.
